skill.name: pytest-mock-guide
skill.description: Practical guide for using pytest-mock to write Python tests with patching, spies, stubs, autospecs, async mocks, and clear assertions.
skill.summary: Learn pytest-mock patterns for reliable Python tests that isolate dependencies and verify behavior.
skill.author: bossjones

security_audit.summary: AI review found the 136 static findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, fenced Python test examples, and ordinary pytest-mock terminology. The files contain documentation only; no executable scripts, network calls, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or destructive instructions were found. Publishable with low risk because examples mention mocked filesystem APIs.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer interpreted Markdown backticks and fenced Python examples as Ruby shell execution. The flagged content documents pytest-mock APIs and does not invoke shells, subprocesses, or command runners. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative locations across both files are Markdown prose, inline code, or Python test snippets. No reviewed context shows executable shell invocation or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Filesystem API Mentions Are Mocking Examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem detections are examples that patch os.remove, os.listdir, os.path.exists, or builtins.open for tests. They are documentation snippets and do not make the skill read, write, delete, or enumerate local files. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged APIs appear inside pytest-mock teaching examples that replace real calls with mocks. No surrounding text instructs the agent to perform real filesystem actions.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity keyword alerts occur in ordinary documentation terms and mock call-order examples, not in cryptographic code or command-and-control behavior. No evidence found for weak cryptographic algorithms, beaconing, payload delivery, or exfiltration. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines shows pytest-mock terminology and sample function names. There are no network destinations, encoded payloads, cryptographic primitives, or covert-control instructions.

content.user_title: Write Better pytest Tests With pytest-mock
content.value_statement: Python tests often become brittle when dependencies are patched inconsistently. This skill gives clear pytest-mock patterns for patching, spying, stubbing, and assertions.
actual_capabilities: Explains the pytest-mock mocker fixture and available fixture scopes.
Shows patching patterns for modules, objects, dictionaries, and multiple attributes.
Covers spies, stubs, autospecs, AsyncMock, MagicMock, and assertion helpers.
Provides examples for async tests, context managers, iterators, callbacks, and call order checks.
Highlights configuration options and common patch-location mistakes.
limitations: Does not install pytest, pytest-mock, or project dependencies.
Does not run tests or inspect a repository by itself.
Examples use placeholder module names that must be adapted to real code.
Focuses on pytest-mock rather than broader test architecture.

use_cases.title: Add Mocking to Unit Tests
use_cases.description: Use patching and stubs to isolate dependencies in Python unit tests.

use_cases.title: Review Test Reliability
use_cases.description: Check whether mocks use autospecs, correct patch targets, and clear assertions.

use_cases.title: Teach pytest-mock Patterns
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain spies, async mocks, and fixture scopes.

prompt_templates.title: Start With mocker
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner unit test using one patched dependency.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use pytest-mock to patch one dependency in a pytest test for this function.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Patch Target
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate test where imports make patch location unclear.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me identify where to patch this dependency with pytest-mock, and explain why that target is correct.

prompt_templates.title: Add Spies and Assertions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate behavior verification without replacing the real function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this test to use mocker.spy and add assertions for calls, arguments, and return values.

prompt_templates.title: Design Advanced Mocking
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced async or multi-dependency test design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design pytest-mock tests for this async workflow using autospecs, stubs, and clear call-order assertions.

output_examples.input: A function calls an external client and writes a status message.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests patching the client where it is imported, replacing the print call, and asserting both interactions.

output_examples.input: A reviewer sees a test patching the original module instead of the imported reference.
output_examples.output: The skill explains the patch-location issue and recommends patching the name used by the code under test.

output_examples.input: An async function uses an async context manager and yields items.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines AsyncMock setup, async iterator behavior, and assertions for awaited calls.

best_practices: Patch the name used by the code under test, not only where the dependency was defined.
Use autospec or spec_set when mocks should preserve the real interface.
Assert meaningful calls and outcomes instead of only checking that a mock was touched.
anti_patterns: Patching broad modules when a single imported name is the real dependency.
Using unrestricted mocks that hide typos and interface changes.
Mocking so much behavior that the test no longer verifies useful application logic.

faq.question: What is pytest-mock?
faq.answer: pytest-mock is a pytest plugin that provides the mocker fixture as a convenient wrapper around unittest.mock.

faq.question: Does this skill replace pytest documentation?
faq.answer: No. It provides practical guidance and examples for common pytest-mock tasks.

faq.question: Can it help with async tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers AsyncMock, async stubs, async context managers, and async iterator examples.

faq.question: Does it run my test suite?
faq.answer: No. It explains patterns, but a separate tool or command must run tests.

faq.question: Can it prevent over-mocking?
faq.answer: It can recommend narrower patches, autospecs, and better assertions, but final test design remains your decision.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
